sources.title: Direct parse of references 15-23
facts: Reference 23 claims 103 disease-to-herb entries but contains 100 numbered entries.
Numbers 52, 53 and 54 are absent, and the source order has inversions from 47 to 36 and from 60 to 55; no missing content is inferred or fabricated.

sources.title: FDA 101: Dietary Supplements
facts: Herbal and other dietary supplements can have strong biological effects, interactions and adverse events.
FDA does not approve dietary supplements for safety and effectiveness before sale, and disease treatment or prevention claims require the applicable drug evidence and regulation.
Consumers should discuss use with a health professional and should not assume that natural means safe.

sources.title: Mixing Medications and Dietary Supplements Can Endanger Your Health
facts: Children metabolize substances differently at different ages and can be harmed by supplement and medicine combinations.
A fixed fraction of an adult dose is not an adequate general pediatric dosing contract.

sources.title: Poison Help: What You Can Do
facts: Suspected poisoning should prompt immediate contact with Poison Help rather than waiting for symptoms.
First-aid actions depend on the exposure and should come from a poison expert.

sources.title: Poisoning first aid
facts: Do not induce vomiting unless directed by poison control or a health professional.
Do not try to neutralize a poison or use a cure-all antidote without professional direction.

sources.title: Import Alert 54-10: Products Containing Aristolochic Acid
facts: Aristolochic acid is a potent carcinogen and nephrotoxin associated with irreversible renal damage and renal failure.
The alert names Ma dou ling, Tian xian teng, Qing mu xiang, Guang fang ji and related Aristolochia materials and requires analytical evidence for absence of aristolochic acid.

sources.title: NMPA notices on Guanmutong and six aristolochic-acid medicines
facts: The 2003 notice cancelled the medicinal standard for Guanmutong and required replacement with Mutong.
The 2004 notice cancelled standards for Guangfangji and Qingmuxiang and restricted formulas containing Madouling, Xungufeng, Tianxianteng and Zhushalian.

sources.title: Regulation on Medical Toxic Drugs and current official reproductions
facts: The legal toxic-medicine control list includes arsenic, mercury and other high-risk Chinese medicines such as Pishi/Pishuang, Qingfen and Xionghuang.
This list is a minimum regulatory blocklist and not a complete clinical safety assessment.

sources.title: FDA warning on heavy metal poisoning associated with traditional drug products
facts: Lead, mercury and arsenic exposure can cause kidney, neurologic, gastrointestinal and other serious injury; acute ingestion can be life-threatening.
Consumers with possible exposure or symptoms should contact a health professional or poison center.

sources.title: Pharmacopoeia of the People's Republic of China, 2025 edition
facts: Medicine identity, processing, dosage and precautions require medicine-level comparison with the effective pharmacopoeia and applicable regulation.
A textbook summary or one generic decoction-time rule does not establish product eligibility.

sources.title: Traditional Chinese Medicine: What You Need To Know
facts: Evidence for Chinese herbal products varies and many condition-level studies are mixed or poor quality.
Traditional use or a textbook modern-effect phrase does not validate a disease-specific effectiveness claim.

sources.title: Tetanus clinical care and treatment
facts: Tetanus is a medical emergency requiring hospitalization, medication and supportive care.
A disease-to-herb reverse lookup must not be presented as treatment or delay emergency care.

sources.title: Treatment of Malaria
facts: Malaria is a medical emergency and requires prompt diagnosis and prescription treatment selected by species, location, age, pregnancy status and severity.
A reverse herb list cannot substitute for diagnostic testing or an approved antimalarial regimen.

boundaries: These sources establish safety, regulatory, pediatric, poisoning and evidence boundaries; they do not validate every traditional property or indication in references 15-23.
Complete source preservation and risk tagging are not medicine-by-medicine pharmacopoeia validation.
No dose, administration route, poisoning response, disease treatment, pediatric rule, pregnancy rule or modern efficacy claim becomes runtime eligible in this layer.
